completeness.rationale: Six of seven expected information items were captured: linear memory growth pattern, v2.3 upgrade timing (~1 week ago), 200 active users, day-to-day consistency, restart as workaround, and the v2.3 changelog's notification system changes. The one missing item is the server specs (8GB RAM VM) — the summary explicitly notes 'server details not specified' even though this is in the ground truth environment.

accuracy.rationale: The triage accurately identifies both acceptable diagnostic paths: the v2.3 upgrade as the trigger and the notification system as the likely source, and a per-request resource leak pattern (even computing the same 5,000 objects/hour figure as the ground truth). The hypothesis that per-request listener registrations are never cleaned up matches the actual cause precisely. The ruling-out of connection-level leaks via the ss/netstat check is also accurate and consistent with the ground truth.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked all the critical questions: version/upgrade timing, usage-correlated vs idle growth, connection-level leak check, and profiling/feature-disable options. The only meaningful omission is that server specs (RAM, OS) were never asked — the ground truth environment (8GB RAM, Ubuntu 22.04) was not surfaced. This is a minor gap given how well the functional investigation was conducted.

economy.rationale: All four question turns were purposeful. The websocket connection count check (turn 3) turned out to be a dead end but was a reasonable diagnostic hypothesis and produced useful negative evidence that sharpened the per-request leak theory. It was not wasted — it actively narrowed the problem space. At most one turn could arguably have been folded into another, but none were redundant.

question_quality.rationale: The questions showed genuine diagnostic reasoning at each step. The first question immediately targeted version and change correlation rather than asking generic debugging questions. The second question (idle vs. active) was particularly well-designed — it drew out both the weekend usage data and the reporter's unprompted mention of the v2.3 changelog's notification feature, which was the key diagnostic pivot. The third question tested a specific failure mode (connection leak vs. internal leak) rather than fishing. The fourth question proposed a concrete isolation test and surfaced the Go runtime for profiling tool recommendations.

actionability.rationale: The triage summary is exceptionally actionable: it includes a specific root cause hypothesis, exact pprof commands with flags, a step-by-step reproduction procedure with quantitative expectations, code-level fix guidance (deregister listeners, close channels, remove map entries, use context.Done()), and a proposed test case with specific metrics. A developer could open the notification system diff and run pprof immediately.

notable_strengths: Immediately targeted version and upgrade correlation in the first question, unlocking the key regression signal without any wasted turns
The idle-vs-active question drew out the reporter's unprompted mention of the v2.3 changelog's 'improved real-time notifications' — this was the critical pivot that pointed to the right subsystem
Correctly computed the per-request accumulation rate (200 users × 25 req/hr = 5,000/hr) matching the ground truth's own estimate, demonstrating quantitative diagnostic reasoning
The final summary is developer-ready: pprof commands, a concrete fix approach (deregister on context.Done), and a proposed regression test with numeric bounds
notable_weaknesses: Server specs (8GB RAM, Ubuntu 22.04) were never elicited — the summary explicitly notes 'server details not specified', leaving an expected information item uncaptured

The connection-count check (turn 3) was a reasonable but slightly speculative detour into WebSocket connection leaks before the per-request theory was fully developed; the negative result was useful but could have been avoided with tighter reasoning from the activity-correlation data
most_insightful_question: Does the memory growth correlate with active user activity, or does it climb even when the system is idle? — This question elicited the weekend comparison data confirming usage-correlation, and triggered the reporter to voluntarily mention the v2.3 changelog's 'improved real-time notifications' feature, which was the single most important diagnostic clue in the entire conversation.
missed_opportunities: Ask for server specs (RAM, OS) — the 8GB RAM figure is relevant to understanding when OOM risk becomes critical and was part of the expected extracts
Ask whether pprof endpoints are enabled on the running server before recommending them as a diagnostic tool — this would have been practical given the Go runtime disclosure
